revision.text:
'''Kitabatake Chikafusa''' (北畠親房)(d. [[1354]]) was a Japanese writer of the 14th century who supported the Southern Court in the [[Nanboku-cho]] period, serving as advisor to five [[Emperor of Japan|Emperors]]. Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Daigo of Japan|Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a history of Japan and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto]] clan.
Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[Ashikaga family]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of Shogun and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at Court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous [[Minamoto clan]] than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.
Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of [[Kitabatake Akiie]], Chikafusa's son.
When Go-Daigo died in [[1339]], Chikafusa was under siege in his stronghold in [[Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[Emperor Go-Murakami of Japan|Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors. Though he is most well-known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to Yoshino, the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in [[1354]].
Chikafusa is known as one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival Ashikaga Takauji. Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line.
==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[Shugo]] and [[Jito|Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in [[1190]] altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.
Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[Taiho (era)|Taihō Era]], in [[702]], before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (Court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|Throne]].
In [[1339]], he wrote ''[[Jinno Shotoki|Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', which related the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical Emperors down to Go-Daigo and his successor Go-Murakami. It was intended largely as a guide to the young Emperor Go-Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in [[1343]]. One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.
Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in [[1339]], largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.
==Reference== *Sansom, George (1961). "A History of Japan: 1334-1615." Stanford: Stanford University Press.

revision.text:
'''{{nihongo|Kitabatake Chikafusa|北畠親房|}}'''(d. [[1354]]) was a Japanese writer of the 14th century who supported the Southern Court in the [[Nanboku-cho]] period, serving as advisor to five [[Emperor of Japan|Emperors]]. Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a history of Japan and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].
Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of Shogun and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at Court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.
Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of [[Kitabatake Akiie]], Chikafusa's son.
When Go-Daigo died in [[1339]], Chikafusa was under siege in his stronghold in [[Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors. Though he is most well-known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to Yoshino, the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in [[1354]].
Chikafusa is known as one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival Ashikaga Takauji. Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line.
==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[Shugo]] and [[Jito|Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in [[1190]] altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.
Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[Taiho (era)|Taihō Era]], in [[702]], before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (Court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|Throne]].
In [[1339]], he wrote ''[[Jinno Shotoki|Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', which related the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical Emperors down to Go-Daigo and his successor Go-Murakami. It was intended largely as a guide to the young Emperor Go-Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in [[1343]]. One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.
Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in [[1339]], largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.
==Reference== *Sansom, George (1961). "A History of Japan: 1334-1615." Stanford: Stanford University Press.
[[Category:Japanese historians]]
[[ja:北畠親房]]

revision.comment: In Japan, the nobility (noble class) was synonymous with the samurai class; so Kitabatake ''was'' samurai

In [[1339]], he wrote ''[[Jinno Shotoki|Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', which related the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical Emperors down to Go-Daigo and his successor Go-Murakami. It was intended largely as a guide to the young Emperor Go-Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in [[1343]]. One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.
Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in [[1339]], largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.

'''{{nihongo|Kitabatake Chikafusa|北畠親房|}}'''(d. [[1354]]) was a Japanese writer of the 14th century who supported the Southern Court in the [[Nanboku-cho]] period, serving as advisor to five [[Emperor of Japan|Emperors]]. Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a history of Japan and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].
Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of Shogun and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at Court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.
Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of [[Kitabatake Akiie]], Chikafusa's son.
When Go-Daigo died in [[1339]], Chikafusa was under siege in his stronghold in [[Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors. Though he is most well-known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to Yoshino, the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in [[1354]].
Chikafusa is known as one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival Ashikaga Takauji. Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line.
==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[Shugo]] and [[Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in [[1190]] altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.
Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in [[702]], before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (Court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|Throne]].
In [[1339]], he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', which related the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical Emperors down to Go-Daigo and his successor Go-Murakami. It was intended largely as a guide to the young Emperor Go-Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in [[1343]]. One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.
Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in [[1339]], largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.
==Reference== *Sansom, George (1961). "A History of Japan: 1334-1615." Stanford: Stanford University Press.
[[Category:Japanese historians]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Kuge of Feudal Japan]]

{{Infobox Biography | subject_name = Kitabatake Chikafusa | image_name = 北畠親房.jpg | image_size = 400px | image_caption = Kitabatake Chikafusa, as drawn by Japanese painter Kikuchi Yosai | date_of_birth = [[1293]] | place_of_birth = | date_of_death = [[1354]] | place_of_death = [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino, Nara, Japan]] | occupation = [[Writer]], [[Advisor]] | spouse = | parents = | children = [[Kitabatake Akiie]], [[Kitabatake Akinobu]] }} '''{{nihongo|Kitabatake Chikafusa|北畠親房|}}'''(1293-1354)Paul Varley, "Kitabatake Chikafusa", in ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World'', Ian McGready, ed., New York: Harper Collins, 1995, p. 335. ISBN 0062700855 was a Japanese writer of the 14th century who supported the Southern Court in the [[Nanboku-cho]] period, serving as advisor to five [[Emperor of Japan|Emperors]]. Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a history of Japan and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]]. Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of Shogun and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at Court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court. Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of [[Kitabatake Akiie]], Chikafusa's son. When Go-Daigo died in [[1339]], Chikafusa was under siege in his stronghold in [[Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors. Though he is most well-known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to Yoshino, the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in [[1354]]. Chikafusa is known as one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival Ashikaga Takauji. Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line. ==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[Shugo]] and [[Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in [[1190]] altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government. Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in [[702]], before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (Court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|Throne]]. In [[1339]], he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', which related the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical Emperors down to Go-Daigo and his successor Go-Murakami. It was intended largely as a guide to the young Emperor Go-Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in [[1343]]. One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land. Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in [[1339]], largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials. ==Notes==
==Reference== *Sansom, George (1961). "A History of Japan: 1334-1615." Stanford: Stanford University Press. [[Category:Japanese historians]] [[Category:Japanese writers]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Kuge of Feudal Japan]] [[Category:1293 births]] [[Category:1354 deaths]] [[ja:北畠親房]]

In [[1339]], he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', which related the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical Emperors down to Go-Daigo and his successor Go-Murakami. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials. ==Notes==

{{Infobox Person | name = Kitabatake Chikafusa | image = 北畠親房.jpg | image_size = 400px | caption = Kitabatake Chikafusa, as drawn by Japanese painter Kikuchi Yosai | birth_date = [[1293]] | birth_place = | death_date = [[1354]] | death_place = [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino, Nara, Japan]] | occupation = [[Writer]], [[Advisor]] | spouse = | parents = | children = [[Kitabatake Akiie]], [[Kitabatake Akinobu]] }} '''{{nihongo|Kitabatake Chikafusa|北畠親房|}}'''(1293-1354)Paul Varley, "Kitabatake Chikafusa", in ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World'', Ian McGready, ed., New York: Harper Collins, 1995, p. 335. ISBN 0062700855 was a Japanese [[kuge|court noble]] and writer of the 14th century who supported the Southern Court in the [[Nanboku-cho]] period, serving as advisor to five [[Emperor of Japan|Emperors]]. Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a history of Japan and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]]. Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of Shogun and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at Court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court. Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of [[Kitabatake Akiie]], Chikafusa's son. When Go-Daigo died in [[1339]], Chikafusa was under siege in his stronghold in [[Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors. Though he is most well-known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to Yoshino, the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in [[1354]]. Chikafusa is known as one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival Ashikaga Takauji. Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line. ==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[Shugo]] and [[Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in [[1190]] altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government. Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in [[702]], before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (Court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|Throne]]. In [[1339]], he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', which related the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical Emperors down to Go-Daigo and his successor Go-Murakami. It was intended largely as a guide to the young Emperor Go-Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in [[1343]]. One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land. Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in [[1339]], largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials. ==Notes==

{{Infobox Writer <!-- for more information see [[:Template:Infobox Writer/doc]] --> | name = Kitabatake Chikafusa | image = 北畠親房.jpg | imagesize = | caption = Kitabatake Chikafusa, as drawn by Japanese painter Kikuchi Yosai | pseudonym = | birthdate = 1293 | birthplace = | deathdate = 1354 | deathplace = [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino, Nara, Japan]] | occupation = Writer, counseloror | nationality = | period = | genre = | subject = Japanese history | movement = | spouse = | partner = | children =[[Kitabatake Akiie]], [[Kitabatake Akinobu]] | relatives = | influences = | influenced = | signature = | website = }}
'''{{nihongo|Kitabatake Chikafusa|北畠親房|}}''' ([[1293]] – [[1354]])<ref>Paul Varley. (1995). "Kitabatake Chikafusa", ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World'', p. 335.</ref> was a Japanese [[kuge|court noble]] and writer of the 14th century who supported the Southern Court in the [[Nanboku-cho]] period, serving as advisor to five [[Emperor of Japan|Emperors]]. Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a history of Japan and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].
Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of Shogun and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at Court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.
Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of [[Kitabatake Akiie]], Chikafusa's son.
When Go-Daigo died in [[1339]], Chikafusa was under siege in his stronghold in [[Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors. Though he is most well-known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to Yoshino, the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in [[1354]].
Chikafusa is known as one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival Ashikaga Takauji. Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line.
==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[Shugo]] and [[Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in [[1190]] altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.
Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in [[702]], before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (Court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|Throne]].
In [[1339]], he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', which related the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical Emperors down to Go-Daigo and his successor Go-Murakami. It was intended largely as a guide to the young Emperor Go-Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in [[1343]]. One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.
Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in [[1339]], largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.

Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|Throne]].
In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', which related the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical Emperors down to Go-Daigo and his successor Go-Murakami. It was intended largely as a guide to the young Emperor Go-Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343. One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.
Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.

{{Infobox Writer <!-- for more information see [[:Template:Infobox Writer/doc]] --> | name = Kitabatake Chikafusa | image = 北畠親房.jpg | imagesize = | caption = Kitabatake Chikafusa, as drawn by Japanese painter Kikuchi Yosai | pseudonym = | birthdate = 1293 | birthplace = | deathdate = 1354 | deathplace = [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino, Nara, Japan]] | occupation = Writer, counseloror | nationality = | period = | genre = | subject = Japanese history | movement = | spouse = | partner = | children =[[Kitabatake Akiie]], [[Kitabatake Akinobu]] | relatives = | influences = | influenced = | signature = | website = }}
'''{{nihongo|Kitabatake Chikafusa|北畠親房|}}''' (1293 – 1354)<ref>Paul Varley. (1995). "Kitabatake Chikafusa", ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World'', p. 335.</ref> was a Japanese [[kuge|court noble]] and writer of the 14th century who supported the Southern Court in the [[Nanboku-cho]] period,<ref>Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.''' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&printsec=frontcover&dq=editions:ISBN2706816325&lr=&client=firefox-a#PPA1553,M1 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1553.]</ref> serving as advisor to five [[Emperor of Japan|Emperors]]. Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a history of Japan and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].
Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of Shogun and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at Court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.
Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of [[Kitabatake Akiie]], Chikafusa's son.
When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Chikafusa was under siege in his stronghold in [[Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors. Though he is most well-known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to Yoshino, the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.
Chikafusa is known as one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival Ashikaga Takauji. Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line.
==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[Shugo]] and [[Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in 1190 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.
Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (Court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.

It was intended largely as a guide to the young Emperor Go-Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343. One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.
Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.

{{Infobox Writer <!-- for more information see [[:Template:Infobox Writer/doc]] --> | name = Kitabatake Chikafusa | image = Kitabatake Chikafusa.svg | imagesize = | caption = Kitabatake Chikafusa, as drawn by Japanese painter Kikuchi Yosai | pseudonym = | birthdate = 1293 | birthplace = | deathdate = 1354 | deathplace = [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino, Nara, Japan]] | occupation = Writer, counselor | nationality = | period = | genre = | subject = Japanese history | movement = | spouse = | partner = | children =[[Kitabatake Akiie]], [[Kitabatake Akinobu]] | relatives = | influences = | influenced = | signature = | website = }}
'''{{nihongo|Kitabatake Chikafusa|北畠親房|}}''' (1293 – 1354)<ref>Paul Varley. (1995). "Kitabatake Chikafusa", ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World'', p. 335.</ref> was a Japanese [[kuge|court noble]] and writer of the 14th century who supported the Southern Court in the [[Nanboku-cho]] period,<ref>Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&printsec=frontcover&dq=editions:ISBN2706816325&lr=&client=firefox-a#PPA1553,M1 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1553.]</ref> serving as advisor to five [[Emperor of Japan|Emperors]]. Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a history of Japan and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Sovereiegn and Subject,'' pp. 139-241.</ref>
Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of Shogun and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at Court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.
Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of [[Kitabatake Akiie]], Chikafusa's son.
When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Chikafusa was under siege in his stronghold in [[Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors. Though he is most well-known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to Yoshino, the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.
Chikafusa is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival Ashikaga Takauji. Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line.
==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[Shugo]] and [[Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in 1190 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.
Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (Court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|Throne]].
In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', which related the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical Emperors down to Go-Daigo and his successor Go-Murakami. It was intended largely as a guide to the young Emperor Go-Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343. One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.
Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.

In particular, he disliked [[Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.
Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of [[Kitabatake Akiie]], Chikafusa's son.
When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Chikafusa was under siege in his stronghold in [[Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors. Though he is most well-known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to Yoshino, the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.
Chikafusa is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival Ashikaga Takauji. Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line.
==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[Shugo]] and [[Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in 1190 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.
Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (Court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|Throne]].
In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', which related the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical Emperors down to Go-Daigo and his successor Go-Murakami. It was intended largely as a guide to the young Emperor Go-Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343. One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.
Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.

{{Infobox writer <!-- for more information see [[:Template:Infobox writer/doc]] --> | name = Kitabatake Chikafusa | image = Kitabatake Chikafusa.svg | imagesize = | caption = Kitabatake Chikafusa, as drawn by Japanese painter Kikuchi Yosai | pseudonym = | birth_date = March 8, 1293 | birth_place = | death_date = June 1, 1354 | death_place = [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino, Nara, Japan]] | occupation = Writer, counselor | nationality = | period = | genre = | subject = Japanese history | movement = | spouse = | partner = | children =[[Kitabatake Akiie]], [[Kitabatake Akinobu]] | relatives = | influences = | influenced = | signature = | website = }}
{{Japanese name|Kitabatake}} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Chikafusa'''|北畠 親房||March 8, 1293 – June 1, 1354}}<ref>Paul Varley. (1995). "Kitabatake Chikafusa", ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World'', p. 335.</ref> was a Japanese [[kuge|court noble]] and writer of the 14th century who supported the Southern Court in the [[Nanboku-cho]] period,<ref>Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&printsec=frontcover&dq=editions:ISBN2706816325&lr=&client=firefox-a#PPA1553,M1 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1553.]</ref> serving as advisor to five [[Emperor of Japan|Emperors]]. Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a history of Japan and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Sovereiegn and Subject,'' pp. 139-241.</ref>
Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of Shogun and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at Court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.
Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of [[Kitabatake Akiie]], Chikafusa's son.
When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Chikafusa was under siege in his stronghold in [[Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors. Though he is most well known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to Yoshino, the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.
Chikafusa is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival Ashikaga Takauji. Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line.
==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[Shugo]] and [[Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in 1190 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.
Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (Court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|Throne]].
In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', which related the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical Emperors down to Go-Daigo and his successor Go-Murakami. It was intended largely as a guide to the young Emperor Go-Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343. One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.
Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.

(1995). ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World.'' (H. Paul Varley, "Kitabatake Chikafusa."). New York: [[Harper Collins]]. ISBN 0062700855 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=ffy6HAAACAAJ&dq=A+History+of+Japan,+1334-1615&client=firefox-a ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.''] Stanford: Stanford University Press. 10-ISBN 0-804-70525-9; 13-ISBN 978-0-804-70525-7; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/43483194 OCLC 43483194]

In particular, he disliked [[Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.
Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of [[Kitabatake Akiie]], Chikafusa's son.
When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Chikafusa was under siege in his stronghold in [[Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors. Though he is most well known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to Yoshino, the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.
Chikafusa is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival Ashikaga Takauji. Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line.
==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[Shugo]] and [[Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in 1190 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.
Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (Court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|Throne]].
In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', which related the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical Emperors down to Go-Daigo and his successor Go-Murakami. It was intended largely as a guide to the young Emperor Go-Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343. One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.
Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.

{{Infobox writer <!-- for more information see [[:Template:Infobox writer/doc]] --> | name = Kitabatake Chikafusa | image = Kitabatake Chikafusa.svg | imagesize = | caption = Kitabatake Chikafusa, as drawn by Japanese painter Kikuchi Yosai | pseudonym = | birth_date = March 8, 1293 | birth_place = | death_date = June 1, 1354 | death_place = [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino, Nara, Japan]] | occupation = Writer, counselor | nationality = | period = | genre = | subject = Japanese history | movement = | spouse = | partner = | children =[[Kitabatake Akiie]], [[Kitabatake Akinobu]] | relatives = | influences = | influenced = | signature = | website = }}
{{Japanese name|Kitabatake}} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Chikafusa'''|北畠 親房||March 8, 1293 – June 1, 1354}}<ref>Paul Varley. (1995). "Kitabatake Chikafusa", ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World'', p. 335.</ref> was a Japanese [[kuge|court noble]] and writer of the 14th century who supported the Southern Court in the [[Nanboku-cho]] period,<ref>Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&printsec=frontcover&dq=editions:ISBN 2-7068-1632-5&lr=&client=firefox-a#PPA1553,M1 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1553.]</ref> serving as advisor to five [[Emperor of Japan|Emperors]]. Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a history of Japan and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Sovereiegn and Subject,'' pp. 139-241.</ref>
Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of Shogun and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at Court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.
Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of [[Kitabatake Akiie]], Chikafusa's son.
When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Chikafusa was under siege in his stronghold in [[Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors. Though he is most well known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to Yoshino, the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.
Chikafusa is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival Ashikaga Takauji. Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line.
==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[Shugo]] and [[Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in 1190 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.
Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (Court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|Throne]].
In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', which related the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical Emperors down to Go-Daigo and his successor Go-Murakami. It was intended largely as a guide to the young Emperor Go-Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343. One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.
Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.

{{Japanese name|Kitabatake}} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Chikafusa'''|北畠 親房||March 8, 1293 – June 1, 1354}}<ref>Paul Varley. (1995). "Kitabatake Chikafusa", ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World'', p. 335.</ref> was a Japanese [[kuge|court noble]] and writer of the 14th century who supported the Southern Court in the [[Nanboku-cho]] period,<ref>Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&printsec=frontcover&dq=editions:ISBN 2-7068-1632-5&lr=&client=firefox-a#PPA1553,M1 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1553.]</ref> serving as advisor to five [[Emperor of Japan|Emperors]]. Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a history of Japan and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Sovereiegn and Subject,'' pp. 139-241.</ref>
Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of Shogun and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at Court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.
Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of [[Kitabatake Akiie]], Chikafusa's son.
When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Chikafusa was under siege in his stronghold in [[Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors. Though he is most well known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to Yoshino, the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.
Chikafusa is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival Ashikaga Takauji. Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line.
==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[Shugo]] and [[Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in 1190 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.
Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (Court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|Throne]].
In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', which related the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical Emperors down to Go-Daigo and his successor Go-Murakami. It was intended largely as a guide to the young Emperor Go-Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343. One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.
Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.

Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|Throne]].
In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', which related the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical Emperors down to Go-Daigo and his successor Go-Murakami. It was intended largely as a guide to the young Emperor Go-Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343. One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.
Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.
