{{Infobox writer <!-- for more information see [[:Template:Infobox writer/doc]] --> | name = Kitabatake Chikafusa | image = Kitabatake Chikafusa.svg | imagesize = | caption = Kitabatake Chikafusa, as drawn by Japanese painter Kikuchi Yosai | pseudonym = | birth_date = March 8, 1293 | birth_place = | death_date = {{Death date and age|1354|6|1|1293|3|8}} | death_place = [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino, Nara, Japan]] | occupation = Writer, counselor | nationality = | period = | genre = | subject = Japanese history | movement = | spouse = | partner = | children =[[Kitabatake Akiie]], [[Kitabatake Akinobu]] | relatives = | influences = | influenced = | signature = | website = }}
{{Japanese name|Kitabatake}} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Chikafusa'''|北畠 親房||March 8, 1293 – June 1, 1354}}<ref>Paul Varley. (1995). "Kitabatake Chikafusa", ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World'', p. 335.</ref> was a Japanese [[kuge|court noble]] and writer of the 14th century who supported the Southern Court in the [[Nanboku-cho]] period,<ref>Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&printsec=frontcover&dq=editions:ISBN 2-7068-1632-5&lr=&client=firefox-a#PPA1553,M1 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1553.]</ref> serving as advisor to five [[Emperor of Japan|Emperors]]. Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a history of Japan and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Sovereiegn and Subject,'' pp. 139-241.</ref>
Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of Shogun and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at Court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.
Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of [[Kitabatake Akiie]], Chikafusa's son.
When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Chikafusa was under siege in his stronghold in [[Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors. Though he is most well known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to Yoshino, the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.
Chikafusa is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival Ashikaga Takauji. Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line.
==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[Shugo]] and [[Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in 1190 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.
Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (Court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|Throne]].
In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', which related the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical Emperors down to Go-Daigo and his successor Go-Murakami. It was intended largely as a guide to the young Emperor Go-Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343. One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.
Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.

[http://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&printsec=frontcover&dq=editions:ISBN 2-7068-1632-5&lr=&client=firefox-a#PPA1553,M1 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1553.]</ref><ref name=Sansom2>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=60}}</ref> serving as advisor to five [[Emperor of Japan|Emperors]].<ref name=Sansom2/>{{rp|68}} Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a history of Japan and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Sovereiegn and Subject,'' pp. 139-241.</ref>
Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of Shogun and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at Court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.
Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of [[Kitabatake Akiie]], Chikafusa's son.
When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Chikafusa was under siege in his stronghold in [[Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors. Though he is most well known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to Yoshino, the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.
Chikafusa is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival Ashikaga Takauji. Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line.
==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[Shugo]] and [[Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in 1190 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.
Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (Court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|throne]].
In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', which related the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical emperors down to Daigō II and his successor Murakami II. It was intended largely as a guide to the young Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343. One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.
Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.

It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343.{{rp|67-68}} One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.{{rp|30}} Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.{{rp|68}} ==See also== * [[Abeno Shrine]] * [[Tenryū-ji]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&client=firefox-a ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. ISBN 978-2-7068-1632-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * McGready, Ian, ed. (1995). ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World.'' (H. Paul Varley, "Kitabatake Chikafusa."). New York: [[Harper Collins]]. ISBN 0-06-270085-5 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=0gweAAAAMAAJ&q= ''Sovereiegn and Subject.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1014075}} * [[George Bailey Sansom|Sansom]], George. (1961). [http://books.google.com/books?id=ffy6HAAACAAJ&dq=A+History+of+Japan,+1334-1615&client=firefox-a ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.''] Stanford: Stanford University Press. ISBN 978-0-8047-0525-7; {{OCLC|43483194}} {{Authority control}} {{Persondata | NAME = Kitabatake Chikafusa | ALTERNATIVE NAMES = | SHORT DESCRIPTION = Historian | DATE OF BIRTH = March 8, 1293 | PLACE OF BIRTH = | DATE OF DEATH = June 1, 1354 | PLACE OF DEATH = [[Yoshino District, Nara]], [[Japan]] }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kitabatake, Chikafusa}} [[Category:Japanese historians]] [[Category:Japanese writers]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Kuge of feudal Japan]] [[Category:1293 births]] [[Category:1354 deaths]]

{{Infobox writer | name = Kitabatake Chikafusa | image = Kitabatake Chikafusa.svg | imagesize = | caption = Kitabatake Chikafusa, as drawn by Japanese painter Kikuchi Yosai | pseudonym = | birth_date = March 8, 1293 | birth_place = | death_date = {{Death date and age|1354|6|1|1293|3|8}} | death_place = [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino, Nara, Japan]] | occupation = Writer, counselor | nationality = | period = | genre = | subject = Japanese history | movement = | spouse = | partner = | children =[[Kitabatake Akiie]]
[[Kitabatake Akinobu]]
[[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | relatives = | influences = | influenced = | signature = | website = }} {{Japanese name|Kitabatake}} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Chikafusa'''|北畠 親房||March 8, 1293 – June 1, 1354}}Paul Varley. (1995). "Kitabatake Chikafusa", ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World'', p. 335. was a Japanese [[kuge|court noble]] and writer of the 14th century who supported the Southern Court in the [[Nanboku-cho]] period,Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&printsec=frontcover&dq=editions:ISBN 2-7068-1632-5&lr=&client=firefox-a#PPA1553,M1 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1553.]{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=60}} serving as advisor to five [[Emperor of Japan|Emperors]].{{rp|67-68}} Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a history of Japan and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Sovereiegn and Subject,'' pp. 139-241. Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of Shogun and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at Court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.{{rp|30-31}} Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of [[Kitabatake Akiie]], Chikafusa's son.{{rp|65}} When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Chikafusa was under siege in his stronghold in [[Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors.{{rp|67}} Though he is most well known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to Yoshino, the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.{{rp|69,84,87-88,97}} Chikafusa is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival Ashikaga Takauji.{{rp|97}} Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line. ==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[Shugo]] and [[Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in 1190 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.{{rp|23}} Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (Court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|throne]].{{rp|23}} In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', which related the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical emperors down to Daigō II and his successor Murakami II. It was intended largely as a guide to the young Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343.{{rp|67-68}} One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.{{rp|30}} Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.{{rp|68}} ==See also== * [[Abeno Shrine]] * [[Tenryū-ji]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&client=firefox-a ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. ISBN 978-2-7068-1632-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * McGready, Ian, ed. (1995). ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World.'' (H. Paul Varley, "Kitabatake Chikafusa."). New York: [[Harper Collins]]. ISBN 0-06-270085-5 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=0gweAAAAMAAJ&q= ''Sovereiegn and Subject.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1014075}} * [[George Bailey Sansom|Sansom]], George. (1961). [http://books.google.com/books?id=ffy6HAAACAAJ&dq=A+History+of+Japan,+1334-1615&client=firefox-a ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.''] Stanford: Stanford University Press. It was intended largely as a guide to the young Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.{{rp|68}} ==Legacy== Kitabatake is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival [[Ashikaga Takauji]].{{rp|97–98,102–106}} Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line. ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] (April 2, 1908; posthumous) ==See also== * [[Kitabatake clan]] * [[Abeno Shrine]] * [[Tenryū-ji]] * [[Prince Kaneyoshi]], contemporary ally ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World.'' (H. Paul Varley, "Kitabatake Chikafusa."). New York: [[HarperCollins]]. {{ISBN|0-06-270085-5}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=0gweAAAAMAAJ ''Sovereign and Subject.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1014075}} * [[George Bailey Sansom|Sansom]], George. (1961). [https://books.google.com/books?id=ffy6HAAACAAJ&q=A+History+of+Japan,+1334-1615 ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.''] Stanford: Stanford University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-8047-0525-7}}; {{OCLC|43483194}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kitabatake, Chikafusa}} [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Kuge]] [[Category:1293 births]] [[Category:1354 deaths]] [[Category:Deified Japanese men]] [[Category:14th-century Japanese historians]] [[Category:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]]

{{short description|14th-century Japanese court noble, author, and historian}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} {{family name hatnote|Kitabatake|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox writer | name = Kitabatake Chikafusa | native_name_lang = ja | native_name = 北畠 親房 | image = Kitabatake Chikafusa.svg | imagesize = | caption = Kitabatake Chikafusa, as drawn by Japanese painter Kikuchi Yosai | pseudonym = | birth_date = March 8, 1293 | birth_place = | death_date = {{Death date and age|1354|6|1|1293|3|8}} | death_place = [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino, Nara]], [[Ashikaga shogunate]] | occupation = Writer, counselor | religion = | nationality = | period = | genre = | subject = Japanese history | language = | movement = | spouse = | partner = | children =[[Kitabatake Akiie]]
[[Kitabatake Akinobu]]
[[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | relatives = | influences = | influenced = | signature = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Chikafusa'''|北畠 親房||March 8, 1293 – June 1, 1354}}Paul Varley. (1995). "Kitabatake Chikafusa", ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World'', p. 335. was a Japanese [[kuge|court noble]] and writer of the 14th century who supported the [[Southern Court]] in the [[Nanboku-cho]] period,Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon''], p. 1553. {{ISBN|2-7068-1632-5}}{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334–1615 |url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans |url-access=registration |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |isbn=0804705259 |page=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans/page/60 60]}} serving as advisor to five [[Emperor of Japan|Emperors]].{{rp|67–68}} Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a [[history of Japan]] and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Sovereign and Subject,'' pp. 139–241. ==Politics== Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of ''[[shōgun]]'' and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.{{rp|30–31}} Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of his son [[Kitabatake Akiie|Akiie]].{{rp|65}} When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Kitabatake was under siege in his stronghold in [[Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors.{{rp|67}} Though he is most well known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.{{rp|69,84,87–88,97}} ==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[Shugo]] and [[Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in 1190 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.{{rp|23}} Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|throne]].{{rp|23}} In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', a chronicle which relates the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical emperors down to Daigō II (Emperor Go-Daigo) and his successor Murakami II (Emperor Go-Murakami). It was intended largely as a guide to the young Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343.{{rp|67–68}} One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.{{rp|30}} Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.{{rp|68}} ==Legacy== Kitabatake is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival [[Ashikaga Takauji]].{{rp|97–98,102–106}} Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line. ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] (April 2, 1908; posthumous) ==See also== * [[Kitabatake clan]] * [[Abeno Shrine]] * [[Tenryū-ji]] * [[Prince Kaneyoshi]], contemporary ally ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; {{OCLC|51096469}} * McGready, Ian, ed. (1995). ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World.'' (H. Paul Varley, "Kitabatake Chikafusa."). New York: [[HarperCollins]]. {{ISBN|0-06-270085-5}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=0gweAAAAMAAJ ''Sovereign and Subject.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1014075}} * [[George Bailey Sansom|Sansom]], George. (1961). [https://books.google.com/books?id=ffy6HAAACAAJ&q=A+History+of+Japan,+1334-1615 ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.''] Stanford: Stanford University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-8047-0525-7}}; {{OCLC|43483194}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kitabatake, Chikafusa}} [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Kuge]] [[Category:1293 births]] [[Category:1354 deaths]] [[Category:Deified Japanese men]] [[Category:14th-century Japanese historians]] [[Category:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia1]] [[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia1]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia1]] [[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia1]]

{{short description|14th-century Japanese court noble, author, and historian}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} {{family name hatnote|Kitabatake|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox writer | name = Kitabatake Chikafusa | native_name_lang = ja | native_name = 北畠 親房 | image = Kitabatake Chikafusa.svg | imagesize = | caption = Kitabatake Chikafusa, as drawn by Japanese painter Kikuchi Yosai | pseudonym = | birth_date = March 8, 1293 | birth_place = | death_date = {{Death date and age|1354|6|1|1293|3|8}} | death_place = [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino, Nara]], [[Ashikaga shogunate]] | occupation = Writer, counselor | religion = | nationality = | period = | genre = | subject = Japanese history | language = | movement = | spouse = | partner = | children =[[Kitabatake Akiie]]
[[Kitabatake Akinobu]]
[[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | relatives = | influences = | influenced = | signature = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Chikafusa'''|北畠 親房||March 8, 1293 – June 1, 1354}}Paul Varley. (1995). "Kitabatake Chikafusa", ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World'', p. 335. was a Japanese [[kuge|court noble]] and writer of the 14th century who supported the [[Southern Court]] in the [[Nanboku-cho]] period,Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon''], p. 1553. {{ISBN|2-7068-1632-5}}{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334–1615 |url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans |url-access=registration |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |isbn=0804705259 |page=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans/page/60 60]}} serving as advisor to five [[Emperor of Japan|Emperors]].{{rp|67–68}} Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a [[history of Japan]] and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Sovereign and Subject,'' pp. 139–241. ==Politics== Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of ''[[shōgun]]'' and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.{{rp|30–31}} Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of his son [[Kitabatake Akiie|Akiie]].{{rp|65}} When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Kitabatake was under siege in his stronghold in [[Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors.{{rp|67}} Though he is most well known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.{{rp|69,84,87–88,97}} ==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[Shugo]] and [[Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in 1190 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.{{rp|23}} Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|throne]].{{rp|23}} In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', a chronicle which relates the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical emperors down to Daigō II (Emperor Go-Daigo) and his successor Murakami II (Emperor Go-Murakami). It was intended largely as a guide to the young Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343.{{rp|67–68}} One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.{{rp|30}} Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.{{rp|68}} ==Legacy== Kitabatake is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival [[Ashikaga Takauji]].{{rp|97–98,102–106}} Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line. ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] (April 2, 1908; posthumous) ==See also== * [[Kitabatake clan]] * [[Abeno Shrine]] * [[Tenryū-ji]] * [[Prince Kaneyoshi]], contemporary ally ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; {{OCLC|51096469}} * McGready, Ian, ed. (1995). ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World.'' (H. Paul Varley, "Kitabatake Chikafusa."). New York: [[HarperCollins]]. {{ISBN|0-06-270085-5}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=0gweAAAAMAAJ ''Sovereign and Subject.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1014075}} * [[George Bailey Sansom|Sansom]], George. (1961). [https://books.google.com/books?id=ffy6HAAACAAJ&q=A+History+of+Japan,+1334-1615 ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.''] Stanford: Stanford University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-8047-0525-7}}; {{OCLC|43483194}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kitabatake, Chikafusa}} [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Kuge]] [[Category:1293 births]] [[Category:1354 deaths]] [[Category:Deified Japanese men]] [[Category:14th-century Japanese historians]] [[Category:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[Category:Top level categories]] [[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[Category:Top level categories]] [[Category:Top level categories]] [[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[Category:Top level categories]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia01]]

"Kitabatake Chikafusa", ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World'', p. 335. was a Japanese [[kuge|court noble]] and writer of the 14th century who supported the [[Southern Court]] in the [[Nanboku-cho]] period,Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon''], p. 1553. {{ISBN|2-7068-1632-5}}{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334–1615 |url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans |url-access=registration |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |isbn=0804705259 |page=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans/page/60 60]}} serving as advisor to five [[Emperor of Japan|Emperors]].{{rp|67–68}} Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a [[history of Japan]] and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Sovereign and Subject,'' pp. 139–241. ==Politics== Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of ''[[shōgun]]'' and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.{{rp|30–31}} Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of his son [[Kitabatake Akiie|Akiie]].{{rp|65}} When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Kitabatake was under siege in his stronghold in [[Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors.{{rp|67}} Though he is most well known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.{{rp|69,84,87–88,97}} ==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[Shugo]] and [[Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in 1190 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.{{rp|23}} Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|throne]].{{rp|23}} In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', a chronicle which relates the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical emperors down to Daigō II (Emperor Go-Daigo) and his successor Murakami II (Emperor Go-Murakami). It was intended largely as a guide to the young Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343.{{rp|67–68}} One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.{{rp|30}} Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.{{rp|68}} ==Legacy== Kitabatake is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival [[Ashikaga Takauji]].{{rp|97–98,102–106}} Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line. ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] (April 2, 1908; posthumous) ==See also== * [[Kitabatake clan]] * [[Abeno Shrine]] * [[Tenryū-ji]] * [[Prince Kaneyoshi]], contemporary ally ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; {{OCLC|51096469}} * McGready, Ian, ed. (1995). ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World.'' (H. Paul Varley, "Kitabatake Chikafusa."). New York: [[HarperCollins]]. {{ISBN|0-06-270085-5}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=0gweAAAAMAAJ ''Sovereign and Subject.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1014075}} * [[George Bailey Sansom|Sansom]], George. (1961). [https://books.google.com/books?id=ffy6HAAACAAJ&q=A+History+of+Japan,+1334-1615 ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.''] Stanford: Stanford University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-8047-0525-7}}; {{OCLC|43483194}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kitabatake, Chikafusa}} [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Kuge]] [[Category:1293 births]] [[Category:1354 deaths]] [[Category:Deified Japanese men]] [[Category:14th-century Japanese historians]] [[Category:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia29]] [[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia29]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia01]]

In particular, he disliked [[Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.{{rp|30–31}} Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of his son [[Kitabatake Akiie|Akiie]].{{rp|65}} When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Kitabatake was under siege in his stronghold in [[Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors.{{rp|67}} Though he is most well known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.{{rp|69,84,87–88,97}} ==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[Shugo]] and [[Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in 1190 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.{{rp|23}} Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|throne]].{{rp|23}} In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', a chronicle which relates the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical emperors down to Daigō II (Emperor Go-Daigo) and his successor Murakami II (Emperor Go-Murakami). It was intended largely as a guide to the young Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343.{{rp|67–68}} One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.{{rp|30}} Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.{{rp|68}} ==Legacy== Kitabatake is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival [[Ashikaga Takauji]].{{rp|97–98,102–106}} Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line. ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] (April 2, 1908; posthumous) ==See also== * [[Kitabatake clan]] * [[Abeno Shrine]] * [[Tenryū-ji]] * [[Prince Kaneyoshi]], contemporary ally ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World.'' (H. Paul Varley, "Kitabatake Chikafusa."). New York: [[HarperCollins]]. {{ISBN|0-06-270085-5}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343.{{rp|67–68}} One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.{{rp|30}} Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.{{rp|68}} ==Legacy== Kitabatake is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival [[Ashikaga Takauji]].{{rp|97–98,102–106}} Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line. ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] (April 2, 1908; posthumous) ==See also== * [[Kitabatake clan]] * [[Abeno Shrine]] * [[Tenryū-ji]] * [[Prince Kaneyoshi]], contemporary ally ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; {{OCLC|51096469}} * McGready, Ian, ed. (1995). ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World.'' (H. Paul Varley, "Kitabatake Chikafusa."). New York: [[HarperCollins]]. {{ISBN|0-06-270085-5}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=0gweAAAAMAAJ ''Sovereign and Subject.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1014075}} * [[George Bailey Sansom|Sansom]], George. (1961). [https://books.google.com/books?id=ffy6HAAACAAJ&q=A+History+of+Japan,+1334-1615 ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.''] Stanford: Stanford University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-8047-0525-7}}; {{OCLC|43483194}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kitabatake, Chikafusa}} [[category:Minamoto clan]] [[category:Kuge]] [[category:1293 births]] [[category:1354 deaths]] [[category:Deified Japanese men]] [[category:14th-century Japanese historians]] [[category:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[category:pages and categories from WikipediaWikipedia]]

{{short description|14th-century Japanese court noble, author, and historian}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} {{family name hatnote|Kitabatake|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox writer | name = Kitabatake Chikafusa | native_name_lang = ja | native_name = 北畠 親房 | image = Kitabatake Chikafusa.svg | imagesize = | caption = Kitabatake Chikafusa, as drawn by Japanese painter Kikuchi Yosai | pseudonym = | birth_date = March 8, 1293 | birth_place = | death_date = {{Death date and age|1354|6|1|1293|3|8}} | death_place = [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino, Nara]], [[Ashikaga shogunate]] | occupation = Writer, counselor | religion = | nationality = | period = | genre = | subject = Japanese history | language = | movement = | spouse = | partner = | children =[[Kitabatake Akiie]]
[[Kitabatake Akinobu]]
[[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | relatives = | influences = | influenced = | signature = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Chikafusa'''|北畠 親房||March 8, 1293 – June 1, 1354}}Paul Varley. (1995). "Kitabatake Chikafusa", ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World'', p. 335. was a Japanese [[kuge|court noble]] and writer of the 14th century who supported the [[Southern Court]] in the [[Nanboku-cho]] period,Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon''], p. 1553. {{ISBN|2-7068-1632-5}}{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334–1615 |url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans |url-access=registration |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |isbn=0804705259 |page=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans/page/60 60]}} serving as advisor to five [[Emperor of Japan|Emperors]].{{rp|67–68}} Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a [[history of Japan]] and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Sovereign and Subject,'' pp. 139–241. ==Politics== Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of ''[[shōgun]]'' and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.{{rp|30–31}} Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of his son [[Kitabatake Akiie|Akiie]].{{rp|65}} When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Kitabatake was under siege in his stronghold in [[Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors.{{rp|67}} Though he is most well known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.{{rp|69,84,87–88,97}} ==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[Shugo]] and [[Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in 1190 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.{{rp|23}} Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|throne]].{{rp|23}} In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', a chronicle which relates the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical emperors down to Daigō II (Emperor Go-Daigo) and his successor Murakami II (Emperor Go-Murakami). It was intended largely as a guide to the young Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343.{{rp|67–68}} One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.{{rp|30}} Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.{{rp|68}} ==Legacy== Kitabatake is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival [[Ashikaga Takauji]].{{rp|97–98,102–106}} Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line. ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] (April 2, 1908; posthumous) ==See also== * [[Kitabatake clan]] * [[Abeno Shrine]] * [[Tenryū-ji]] * [[Prince Kaneyoshi]], contemporary ally ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; {{OCLC|51096469}} * McGready, Ian, ed. (1995). ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World.'' (H. Paul Varley, "Kitabatake Chikafusa."). New York: [[HarperCollins]]. {{ISBN|0-06-270085-5}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=0gweAAAAMAAJ ''Sovereign and Subject.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1014075}} * [[George Bailey Sansom|Sansom]], George. (1961). [https://books.google.com/books?id=ffy6HAAACAAJ&q=A+History+of+Japan,+1334-1615 ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.''] Stanford: Stanford University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-8047-0525-7}}; {{OCLC|43483194}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kitabatake, Chikafusa}} [[category:Minamoto clan]] [[category:Kuge]] [[category:1293 births]] [[category:1354 deaths]] [[category:Deified Japanese men]] [[category:14th-century Japanese historians]] [[category:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[category:pages and categories from WikipediaWikipedia]] [[Category:kk]]

''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World.'' (H. Paul Varley, "Kitabatake Chikafusa."). New York: [[HarperCollins]]. {{ISBN|0-06-270085-5}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=0gweAAAAMAAJ ''Sovereign and Subject.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1014075}} * [[George Bailey Sansom|Sansom]], George. (1961). {{short description|14th-century Japanese court noble, author, and historian}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} {{family name hatnote|Kitabatake|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox writer | name = Kitabatake Chikafusa | native_name_lang = ja | native_name = 北畠 親房 | image = Kitabatake Chikafusa.svg | imagesize = | caption = Kitabatake Chikafusa, as drawn by Japanese painter Kikuchi Yosai | pseudonym = | birth_date = March 8, 1293 | birth_place = | death_date = {{Death date and age|1354|6|1|1293|3|8}} | death_place = [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino, Nara]], [[Ashikaga shogunate]] | occupation = Writer, counselor | religion = | nationality = | period = | genre = | subject = Japanese history | language = | movement = | spouse = | partner = | children =[[Kitabatake Akiie]]
[[Kitabatake Akinobu]]
[[Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | relatives = | influences = | influenced = | signature = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Chikafusa'''|北畠 親房||March 8, 1293 – June 1, 1354}}Paul Varley. (1995). "Kitabatake Chikafusa", ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World'', p. 335. was a Japanese [[kuge|court noble]] and writer of the 14th century who supported the [[Southern Court]] in the [[Nanboku-cho]] period,Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon''], p. 1553. {{ISBN|2-7068-1632-5}}{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334–1615 |url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans |url-access=registration |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |isbn=0804705259 |page=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans/page/60 60]}} serving as advisor to five [[Emperor of Japan|Emperors]].{{rp|67–68}} Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a [[history of Japan]] and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Sovereign and Subject,'' pp. 139–241. ==Politics== Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of ''[[shōgun]]'' and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.{{rp|30–31}} Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of his son [[Kitabatake Akiie|Akiie]].{{rp|65}} When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Kitabatake was under siege in his stronghold in [[Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors.{{rp|67}} Though he is most well known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.{{rp|69,84,87–88,97}} ==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[Shugo]] and [[Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in 1190 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.{{rp|23}} Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[Emperor of Japan|throne]].{{rp|23}} In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', a chronicle which relates the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical emperors down to Daigō II (Emperor Go-Daigo) and his successor Murakami II (Emperor Go-Murakami). It was intended largely as a guide to the young Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343.{{rp|67–68}} One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.{{rp|30}} Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.{{rp|68}} ==Legacy== Kitabatake is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival [[Ashikaga Takauji]].{{rp|97–98,102–106}} Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line. ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] (April 2, 1908; posthumous) ==See also== * [[Kitabatake clan]] * [[Abeno Shrine]] * [[Tenryū-ji]] * [[Prince Kaneyoshi]], contemporary ally ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; {{OCLC|51096469}} * McGready, Ian, ed. (1995). ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World.'' (H. Paul Varley, "Kitabatake Chikafusa."). New York: [[HarperCollins]]. {{ISBN|0-06-270085-5}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=0gweAAAAMAAJ ''Sovereign and Subject.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1014075}} * [[George Bailey Sansom|Sansom]], George. (1961). [https://books.google.com/books?id=ffy6HAAACAAJ&q=A+History+of+Japan,+1334-1615 ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.''] Stanford: Stanford University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-8047-0525-7}}; {{OCLC|43483194}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kitabatake, Chikafusa}} [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Kuge]] [[Category:1293 births]] [[Category:1354 deaths]] [[Category:Deified Japanese men]] [[Category:14th-century Japanese historians]] [[Category:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:ddk]] [[Category:kk]]
