"Kitabatake Chikafusa", ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World'', p. 335. was a Japanese [[kuge|court noble]] and writer of the 14th century who supported the [[Southern Court]] in the [[:en:Nanboku-cho|Nanboku-cho]] period,Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon''], p. 1553. {{ISBN|2-7068-1632-5}}{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334–1615 |url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans |url-access=registration |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |isbn=0804705259 |page=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans/page/60 60]}} serving as advisor to five [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperors]].{{rp|67–68}} Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a [[:en:History of Japan|History of Japan]] and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Sovereign and Subject,'' pp. 139–241. ==Politics== Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.{{rp|30–31}} Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of his son [[:en:Kitabatake Akiie|Akiie]].{{rp|65}} When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Kitabatake was under siege in his stronghold in [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[:en:Emperor Go-Murakami|Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors.{{rp|67}} Though he is most well known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.{{rp|69,84,87–88,97}} ==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] and [[:en:Jitō|Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in dd90 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.{{rp|23}} Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|throne]].{{rp|23}} In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', a chronicle which relates the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical emperors down to Daigō II (Emperor Go-Daigo) and his successor Murakami II (Emperor Go-Murakami). It was intended largely as a guide to the young Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343.{{rp|67–68}} One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.{{rp|30}} Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.{{rp|68}} ==Legacy== Kitabatake is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]].{{rp|97–98,102–106}} Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line. ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] (April 2, 1908; posthumous) ==See also== * [[Kitabatake clan]] * [[Abeno Shrine]] * [[Tenryū-ji]] * [[:en:Prince Kaneyoshi|Prince Kaneyoshi]], contemporary ally ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; {{OCLC|51096469}} * McGready, Ian, ed. (1995). ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World.'' (H. Paul Varley, "Kitabatake Chikafusa."). New York: [[:en:HarperCollins|HarperCollins]]. {{ISBN|0-06-270085-5}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=0gweAAAAMAAJ ''Sovereign and Subject.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1014075}} * [[:en:George Bailey Sansom|Sansom]], George. (1961). [https://books.google.com/books?id=ffy6HAAACAAJ&q=A+History+of+Japan,+1334-1615 ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.''] Stanford: Stanford University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-8047-0525-7}}; {{OCLC|43483194}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Kuge]] [[Category:1354 deaths]] [[Category:Deified Japanese men]] [[Category:14th-century Japanese historians]] [[Category:dd 6 4]] }} [[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[arz:كيتاباتاكى شيكافوسا]] [[de:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[es:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[fa:کیتاباتاکه چیکافوسا]] [[fr:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[it:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[ja:北畠親房]] [[ko:기타바타케 지카후사]] [[pl:Chikafusa Kitabatake]] [[pt:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[zh:北畠親房]] {{wikidata link|Q683792}}

He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[:en:Emperor Go-Murakami|Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors.{{rp|67}} Though he is most well known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.{{rp|69,84,87–88,97}} ==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] and [[:en:Jitō|Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in dd90 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.{{rp|23}} Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|throne]].{{rp|23}} In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', a chronicle which relates the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical emperors down to Daigō II (Emperor Go-Daigo) and his successor Murakami II (Emperor Go-Murakami). It was intended largely as a guide to the young Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343.{{rp|67–68}} One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.{{rp|30}} Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.{{rp|68}} ==Legacy== Kitabatake is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]].{{rp|97–98,102–106}} Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line. ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] (April 2, 1908; posthumous) ==See also== * [[Kitabatake clan]] * [[Abeno Shrine]] * [[Tenryū-ji]] * [[:en:Prince Kaneyoshi|Prince Kaneyoshi]], contemporary ally ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (1995). "Kitabatake Chikafusa", ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World'', p. 335. was a Japanese [[kuge|court noble]] and writer of the 14th century who supported the [[Southern Court]] in the [[:en:Nanboku-cho|Nanboku-cho]] period,Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon''], p. 1553. {{ISBN|2-7068-1632-5}}{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334–1615 |url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans |url-access=registration |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |isbn=0804705259 |page=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans/page/60 60]}} serving as advisor to five [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperors]].{{rp|67–68}} Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a [[:en:History of Japan|History of Japan]] and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Sovereign and Subject,'' pp. 139–241. ==Politics== Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.{{rp|30–31}} Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of his son [[:en:Kitabatake Akiie|Akiie]].{{rp|65}} When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Kitabatake was under siege in his stronghold in [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[:en:Emperor Go-Murakami|Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors.{{rp|67}} Though he is most well known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.{{rp|69,84,87–88,97}} ==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] and [[:en:Jitō|Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in dd90 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.{{rp|23}} Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|throne]].{{rp|23}} In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', a chronicle which relates the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical emperors down to Daigō II (Emperor Go-Daigo) and his successor Murakami II (Emperor Go-Murakami). It was intended largely as a guide to the young Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343.{{rp|67–68}} One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.{{rp|30}} Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.{{rp|68}} ==Legacy== Kitabatake is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]].{{rp|97–98,102–106}} Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line. ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] (April 2, 1908; posthumous) ==See also== * [[Kitabatake clan]] * [[Abeno Shrine]] * [[Tenryū-ji]] * [[:en:Prince Kaneyoshi|Prince Kaneyoshi]], contemporary ally ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' [[arz:كيتاباتاكى شيكافوسا]][[de:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[es:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[fa:کیتاباتاکه چیکافوسا]][[fr:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[id:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[it:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[ja:北畠親房]][[ko:기타바타케 지카후사]][[pl:Chikafusa Kitabatake]][[pt:Kitabatake Chikafusa]][[zh:北畠親房]] {{Infobox person|image=File:Kitabatake Chikafusa.svg|birth_date=1293; {{birth date|1293|3|8}}|death_date={{death date|1354|5|10}}; {{death date|1354|6|1}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|occupation={{ill|historian|WD=Q201788}}; {{ill|military personnel|WD=Q47064}}; {{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}}; {{ill|writer|WD=Q36180}}|title={{ill|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706}}; {{ill|Jugō|WD=Q10895712}}; {{ill|Mutsu no Azechi|WD=Q11658599}}; {{ill|jun-daijin|WD=Q11395386}}|children={{ill|Kitabatake Akiie|WD=Q1036864}}; {{ill|Kitabatake Akiko|WD=Q106687761}}; {{ill|Kitabatake Akinobu|WD=Q11403867}}; {{ill|Kitabatake Akiyoshi|WD=Q11403872}}; {{ill|Q110795582|WD=Q110795582}}|father={{ill|Q17220678|WD=Q17220678}}|family={{ill|Kitabatake clan|WD=Q11403853}}|notable_works={{ill|Jinnō Shōtōki|WD=Q1049788}}|organization={{ill|Nochi no Sanbō|WD=Q11488300}}|native_name=北畠 親房|given_name={{ill|Chikafusa|WD=Q125373333}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q683792]] Wikidata name: Kitabatake Chikafusa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q683792]] Wikidata name: Kitabatake Chikafusa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|14th-century Japanese court noble, author, and historian}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} {{family name hatnote|Kitabatake|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox writer | name = Kitabatake Chikafusa | native_name_lang = ja | native_name = 北畠 親房 | image = Kitabatake Chikafusa.svg | imagesize = | caption = Kitabatake Chikafusa, as drawn by Japanese painter Kikuchi Yosai | pseudonym = | birth_date = March 8, 1293 | birth_place = | death_date = {{Death date and age|1354|6|1|1293|3|8}} | death_place = [[:en:Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino, Nara]], [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]] | occupation = Writer, counselor | religion = | nationality = | period = | genre = | subject = Japanese history | language = | movement = | spouse = | partner = | children =[[:en:Kitabatake Akiie|Kitabatake Akiie]]
[[:en:Kitabatake Akinobu|Kitabatake Akinobu]]
[[:en:Kitabatake Akiyoshi|Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | relatives = | influences = | influenced = | signature = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Chikafusa'''|北畠 親房||March 8, 1293 – June 1, 1354}}Paul Varley. (1995). "Kitabatake Chikafusa", ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World'', p. 335. was a Japanese [[kuge|court noble]] and writer of the 14th century who supported the [[Southern Court]] in the [[:en:Nanboku-cho|Nanboku-cho]] period,Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon''], p. 1553. {{ISBN|2-7068-1632-5}}{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334–1615 |url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans |url-access=registration |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |isbn=0804705259 |page=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans/page/60 60]}} serving as advisor to five [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperors]].{{rp|67–68}} Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a [[:en:History of Japan|History of Japan]] and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Sovereign and Subject,'' pp. 139–241. ==Politics== Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.{{rp|30–31}} Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of his son [[:en:Kitabatake Akiie|Akiie]].{{rp|65}} When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Kitabatake was under siege in his stronghold in [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[:en:Emperor Go-Murakami|Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors.{{rp|67}} Though he is most well known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.{{rp|69,84,87–88,97}} ==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] and [[:en:Jitō|Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in dd90 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.{{rp|23}} Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|throne]].{{rp|23}} In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', a chronicle which relates the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical emperors down to Daigō II (Emperor Go-Daigo) and his successor Murakami II (Emperor Go-Murakami). It was intended largely as a guide to the young Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343.{{rp|67–68}} One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.{{rp|30}} Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.{{rp|68}} ==Legacy== Kitabatake is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]].{{rp|97–98,102–106}} Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line. ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] (April 2, 1908; posthumous) ==See also== * [[Kitabatake clan]] * [[Abeno Shrine]] * [[Tenryū-ji]] * [[:en:Prince Kaneyoshi|Prince Kaneyoshi]], contemporary ally ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; {{OCLC|51096469}} * McGready, Ian, ed. (1995). ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World.'' (H. Paul Varley, "Kitabatake Chikafusa."). New York: [[:en:HarperCollins|HarperCollins]]. {{ISBN|0-06-270085-5}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=0gweAAAAMAAJ ''Sovereign and Subject.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1014075}} * [[:en:George Bailey Sansom|Sansom]], George. (1961). [https://books.google.com/books?id=ffy6HAAACAAJ&q=A+History+of+Japan,+1334-1615 ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.''] Stanford: Stanford University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-8047-0525-7}}; {{OCLC|43483194}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Kuge]] [[Category:1354 deaths]] [[Category:Deified Japanese men]] [[Category:14th-century Japanese historians]] [[Category:dd 6 4]] }} [[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[arz:كيتاباتاكى شيكافوسا]] [[de:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[es:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[fa:کیتاباتاکه چیکافوسا]] [[fr:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[it:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[ja:北畠親房]] [[ko:기타바타케 지카후사]] [[pl:Chikafusa Kitabatake]] [[pt:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[zh:北畠親房]] {{wikidata link|Q683792}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[:en:Kitabatake Akinobu|Kitabatake Akinobu]]
[[:en:Kitabatake Akiyoshi|Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | relatives = | influences = | influenced = | signature = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Chikafusa'''|北畠 親房||March 8, 1293 – June 1, 1354}}Paul Varley. (1995). "Kitabatake Chikafusa", ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World'', p. 335. was a Japanese [[kuge|court noble]] and writer of the 14th century who supported the [[Southern Court]] in the [[:en:Nanboku-cho|Nanboku-cho]] period,Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon''], p. 1553. {{ISBN|2-7068-1632-5}}{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334–1615 |url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans |url-access=registration |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |isbn=0804705259 |page=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans/page/60 60]}} serving as advisor to five [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperors]].{{rp|67–68}} Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a [[:en:History of Japan|History of Japan]] and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Sovereign and Subject,'' pp. 139–241. ==Politics== Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.{{rp|30–31}} Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of his son [[:en:Kitabatake Akiie|Akiie]].{{rp|65}} When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Kitabatake was under siege in his stronghold in [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[:en:Emperor Go-Murakami|Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors.{{rp|67}} Though he is most well known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.{{rp|69,84,87–88,97}} ==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] and [[:en:Jitō|Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in dd90 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.{{rp|23}} Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|throne]].{{rp|23}} In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', a chronicle which relates the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical emperors down to Daigō II (Emperor Go-Daigo) and his successor Murakami II (Emperor Go-Murakami). It was intended largely as a guide to the young Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343.{{rp|67–68}} One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.{{rp|30}} Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.{{rp|68}} ==Legacy== Kitabatake is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]].{{rp|97–98,102–106}} Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line. ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] (April 2, 1908; posthumous) ==See also== * [[Kitabatake clan]] * [[Abeno Shrine]] * [[Tenryū-ji]] * [[:en:Prince Kaneyoshi|Prince Kaneyoshi]], contemporary ally ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; {{OCLC|51096469}} * McGready, Ian, ed. (1995). ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World.'' (H. Paul Varley, "Kitabatake Chikafusa."). New York: [[:en:HarperCollins|HarperCollins]]. {{ISBN|0-06-270085-5}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=0gweAAAAMAAJ ''Sovereign and Subject.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1014075}} * [[:en:George Bailey Sansom|Sansom]], George. (1961). [https://books.google.com/books?id=ffy6HAAACAAJ&q=A+History+of+Japan,+1334-1615 ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.''] Stanford: Stanford University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-8047-0525-7}}; {{OCLC|43483194}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Kuge]] [[Category:1354 deaths]] [[Category:Deified Japanese men]] [[Category:14th-century Japanese historians]] [[Category:dd 6 4]] }} [[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[arz:كيتاباتاكى شيكافوسا]] [[de:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[es:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[fa:کیتاباتاکه چیکافوسا]] [[fr:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[it:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[ja:北畠親房]] [[ko:기타바타케 지카후사]] [[pl:Chikafusa Kitabatake]] [[pt:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[zh:北畠親房]] {{wikidata link|Q683792}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

{{ill|Kitabatake Akiko|WD=Q106687761}}; {{ill|Kitabatake Akinobu|WD=Q11403867}}; {{ill|Kitabatake Akiyoshi|WD=Q11403872}}; {{ill|Q110795582|WD=Q110795582}}|father={{ill|Q17220678|WD=Q17220678}}|family={{ill|Kitabatake clan|WD=Q11403853}}|notable_works={{ill|Jinnō Shōtōki|WD=Q1049788}}|organization={{ill|Nochi no Sanbō|WD=Q11488300}}|native_name=北畠 親房|given_name={{ill|Chikafusa|WD=Q125373333}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q683792]] Wikidata name: Kitabatake Chikafusa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q683792]] Wikidata name: Kitabatake Chikafusa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|14th-century Japanese court noble, author, and historian}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} {{family name hatnote|Kitabatake|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox writer | name = Kitabatake Chikafusa | native_name_lang = ja | native_name = 北畠 親房 | image = Kitabatake Chikafusa.svg | imagesize = | caption = Kitabatake Chikafusa, as drawn by Japanese painter Kikuchi Yosai | pseudonym = | birth_date = March 8, 1293 | birth_place = | death_date = {{Death date and age|1354|6|1|1293|3|8}} | death_place = [[:en:Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino, Nara]], [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]] | occupation = Writer, counselor | religion = | nationality = | period = | genre = | subject = Japanese history | language = | movement = | spouse = | partner = | children =[[:en:Kitabatake Akiie|Kitabatake Akiie]]
[[:en:Kitabatake Akinobu|Kitabatake Akinobu]]
[[:en:Kitabatake Akiyoshi|Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | relatives = | influences = | influenced = | signature = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Chikafusa'''|北畠 親房||March 8, 1293 – June 1, 1354}}Paul Varley. (1995). "Kitabatake Chikafusa", ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World'', p. 335. was a Japanese [[kuge|court noble]] and writer of the 14th century who supported the [[Southern Court]] in the [[:en:Nanboku-cho|Nanboku-cho]] period,Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon''], p. 1553. {{ISBN|2-7068-1632-5}}{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334–1615 |url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans |url-access=registration |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |isbn=0804705259 |page=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans/page/60 60]}} serving as advisor to five [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperors]].{{rp|67–68}} Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a [[:en:History of Japan|History of Japan]] and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Sovereign and Subject,'' pp. 139–241. ==Politics== Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.{{rp|30–31}} Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of his son [[:en:Kitabatake Akiie|Akiie]].{{rp|65}} When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Kitabatake was under siege in his stronghold in [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[:en:Emperor Go-Murakami|Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors.{{rp|67}} Though he is most well known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.{{rp|69,84,87–88,97}} ==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] and [[:en:Jitō|Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in dd90 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.{{rp|23}} Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|throne]].{{rp|23}} In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', a chronicle which relates the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical emperors down to Daigō II (Emperor Go-Daigo) and his successor Murakami II (Emperor Go-Murakami). It was intended largely as a guide to the young Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343.{{rp|67–68}} One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.{{rp|30}} Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.{{rp|68}} ==Legacy== Kitabatake is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]].{{rp|97–98,102–106}} Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line. ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] (April 2, 1908; posthumous) ==See also== * [[Kitabatake clan]] * [[Abeno Shrine]] * [[Tenryū-ji]] * [[:en:Prince Kaneyoshi|Prince Kaneyoshi]], contemporary ally ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; {{OCLC|51096469}} * McGready, Ian, ed. (1995). ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World.'' (H. Paul Varley, "Kitabatake Chikafusa."). New York: [[:en:HarperCollins|HarperCollins]]. {{ISBN|0-06-270085-5}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=0gweAAAAMAAJ ''Sovereign and Subject.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1014075}} * [[:en:George Bailey Sansom|Sansom]], George. (1961). [https://books.google.com/books?id=ffy6HAAACAAJ&q=A+History+of+Japan,+1334-1615 ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.''] Stanford: Stanford University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-8047-0525-7}}; {{OCLC|43483194}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Kuge]] [[Category:1354 deaths]] [[Category:Deified Japanese men]] [[Category:14th-century Japanese historians]] [[Category:dd 6 4]] }} [[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[arz:كيتاباتاكى شيكافوسا]] [[de:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[es:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[fa:کیتاباتاکه چیکافوسا]] [[fr:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[it:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[ja:北畠親房]] [[ko:기타바타케 지카후사]] [[pl:Chikafusa Kitabatake]] [[pt:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[zh:北畠親房]] {{wikidata link|Q683792}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

(2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon''], p. 1553. {{ISBN|2-7068-1632-5}}{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334–1615 |url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans |url-access=registration |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |isbn=0804705259 |page=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans/page/60 60]}} serving as advisor to five [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperors]].{{rp|67–68}} Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. (1962). ''Sovereign and Subject,'' pp. 139–241. ==Politics== Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. In particular, he disliked [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.{{rp|30–31}} Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.{{rp|69,84,87–88,97}} ==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] and [[:en:Jitō|Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in dd90 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.{{rp|23}} Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|throne]].{{rp|23}} In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', a chronicle which relates the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical emperors down to Daigō II (Emperor Go-Daigo) and his successor Murakami II (Emperor Go-Murakami). It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343.{{rp|67–68}} One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.{{rp|30}} Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.{{rp|68}} ==Legacy== Kitabatake is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]].{{rp|97–98,102–106}} Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line. ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] (April 2, 1908; posthumous) ==See also== * [[Kitabatake clan]] * [[Abeno Shrine]] * [[Tenryū-ji]] * [[:en:Prince Kaneyoshi|Prince Kaneyoshi]], contemporary ally ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' {{ill|Kitabatake Akiko|WD=Q106687761}}; {{ill|Kitabatake Akinobu|WD=Q11403867}}; {{ill|Kitabatake Akiyoshi|WD=Q11403872}}; {{ill|Q110795582|WD=Q110795582}}|father={{ill|Q17220678|WD=Q17220678}}|family={{ill|Kitabatake clan|WD=Q11403853}}|notable_works={{ill|Jinnō Shōtōki|WD=Q1049788}}|organization={{ill|Nochi no Sanbō|WD=Q11488300}}|native_name=北畠 親房|given_name={{ill|Chikafusa|WD=Q125373333}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q683792]] Wikidata name: Kitabatake Chikafusa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q683792]] Wikidata name: Kitabatake Chikafusa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|14th-century Japanese court noble, author, and historian}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} {{family name hatnote|Kitabatake|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox writer | name = Kitabatake Chikafusa | native_name_lang = ja | native_name = 北畠 親房 | image = Kitabatake Chikafusa.svg | imagesize = | caption = Kitabatake Chikafusa, as drawn by Japanese painter Kikuchi Yosai | pseudonym = | birth_date = March 8, 1293 | birth_place = | death_date = {{Death date and age|1354|6|1|1293|3|8}} | death_place = [[:en:Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino, Nara]], [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]] | occupation = Writer, counselor | religion = | nationality = | period = | genre = | subject = Japanese history | language = | movement = | spouse = | partner = | children =[[:en:Kitabatake Akiie|Kitabatake Akiie]]
[[:en:Kitabatake Akinobu|Kitabatake Akinobu]]
[[:en:Kitabatake Akiyoshi|Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | relatives = | influences = | influenced = | signature = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Chikafusa'''|北畠 親房||March 8, 1293 – June 1, 1354}}Paul Varley. (1995). "Kitabatake Chikafusa", ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World'', p. 335. was a Japanese [[kuge|court noble]] and writer of the 14th century who supported the [[Southern Court]] in the [[:en:Nanboku-cho|Nanboku-cho]] period,Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon''], p. 1553. {{ISBN|2-7068-1632-5}}{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334–1615 |url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans |url-access=registration |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |isbn=0804705259 |page=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans/page/60 60]}} serving as advisor to five [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperors]].{{rp|67–68}} Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a [[:en:History of Japan|History of Japan]] and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Sovereign and Subject,'' pp. 139–241. ==Politics== Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.{{rp|30–31}} Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of his son [[:en:Kitabatake Akiie|Akiie]].{{rp|65}} When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Kitabatake was under siege in his stronghold in [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[:en:Emperor Go-Murakami|Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors.{{rp|67}} Though he is most well known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.{{rp|69,84,87–88,97}} ==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] and [[:en:Jitō|Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in dd90 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.{{rp|23}} Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|throne]].{{rp|23}} In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', a chronicle which relates the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical emperors down to Daigō II (Emperor Go-Daigo) and his successor Murakami II (Emperor Go-Murakami). It was intended largely as a guide to the young Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343.{{rp|67–68}} One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.{{rp|30}} Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.{{rp|68}} ==Legacy== Kitabatake is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]].{{rp|97–98,102–106}} Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line. ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] (April 2, 1908; posthumous) ==See also== * [[Kitabatake clan]] * [[Abeno Shrine]] * [[Tenryū-ji]] * [[:en:Prince Kaneyoshi|Prince Kaneyoshi]], contemporary ally ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; {{OCLC|51096469}} * McGready, Ian, ed. (1995). ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World.'' (H. Paul Varley, "Kitabatake Chikafusa."). New York: [[:en:HarperCollins|HarperCollins]]. {{ISBN|0-06-270085-5}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=0gweAAAAMAAJ ''Sovereign and Subject.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1014075}} * [[:en:George Bailey Sansom|Sansom]], George. (1961). [https://books.google.com/books?id=ffy6HAAACAAJ&q=A+History+of+Japan,+1334-1615 ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.''] Stanford: Stanford University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-8047-0525-7}}; {{OCLC|43483194}} {{draft categories| }} [[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[arz:كيتاباتاكى شيكافوسا]] [[de:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[es:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[fa:کیتاباتاکه چیکافوسا]] [[fr:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[it:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[ja:北畠親房]] [[ko:기타바타케 지카후사]] [[pl:Chikafusa Kitabatake]] [[pt:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[zh:北畠親房]] {{wikidata link|Q683792}} [[Category:1354 deaths]] [[Category:14th-century Japanese historians]] [[Category:dd 6 4]] [[Category:Deified Japanese men]] [[Category:Kuge]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

{{ill|Kitabatake Akiko|WD=Q106687761}}; {{ill|Kitabatake Akinobu|WD=Q11403867}}; {{ill|Kitabatake Akiyoshi|WD=Q11403872}}; {{ill|Q110795582|WD=Q110795582}}|father={{ill|Q17220678|WD=Q17220678}}|family={{ill|Kitabatake clan|WD=Q11403853}}|notable_works={{ill|Jinnō Shōtōki|WD=Q1049788}}|organization={{ill|Nochi no Sanbō|WD=Q11488300}}|native_name=北畠 親房|given_name={{ill|Chikafusa|WD=Q125373333}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q683792]] Wikidata name: Kitabatake Chikafusa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q683792]] Wikidata name: Kitabatake Chikafusa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|14th-century Japanese court noble, author, and historian}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} {{family name hatnote|Kitabatake|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox writer | name = Kitabatake Chikafusa | native_name_lang = ja | native_name = 北畠 親房 | image = Kitabatake Chikafusa.svg | imagesize = | caption = Kitabatake Chikafusa, as drawn by Japanese painter Kikuchi Yosai | pseudonym = | birth_date = March 8, 1293 | birth_place = | death_date = {{Death date and age|1354|6|1|1293|3|8}} | death_place = [[:en:Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino, Nara]], [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]] | occupation = Writer, counselor | religion = | nationality = | period = | genre = | subject = Japanese history | language = | movement = | spouse = | partner = | children =[[:en:Kitabatake Akiie|Kitabatake Akiie]]
[[:en:Kitabatake Akinobu|Kitabatake Akinobu]]
[[:en:Kitabatake Akiyoshi|Kitabatake Akiyoshi]] | relatives = | influences = | influenced = | signature = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Kitabatake Chikafusa'''|北畠 親房||March 8, 1293 – June 1, 1354}}Paul Varley. (1995). "Kitabatake Chikafusa", ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World'', p. 335. was a Japanese [[kuge|court noble]] and writer of the 14th century who supported the [[Southern Court]] in the [[:en:Nanboku-cho|Nanboku-cho]] period,Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon''], p. 1553. {{ISBN|2-7068-1632-5}}{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334–1615 |url=https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans |url-access=registration |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |isbn=0804705259 |page=[https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans/page/60 60]}} serving as advisor to five [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperors]].{{rp|67–68}} Some of his greatest and most famous work was performed during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], under whom he proposed a series of reforms, amounting to a revival or restoration of political and economic systems of several centuries earlier. In addition to authoring a [[:en:History of Japan|History of Japan]] and a number of works defending the right of Go-Daigo's line to the throne, Kitabatake fought in defense of the Southern Court as a member of the Murakami branch of the [[Minamoto clan]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Sovereign and Subject,'' pp. 139–241. ==Politics== Kitabatake, in his writings, held a strong distaste for the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]], the ruling family at the time who held the position of ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' and maintained a rival Imperial court known as the [[Northern Court (Japan)|Northern Court]]. This disdain came not only because they were warriors rather than nobles, who were clumsy and uncouth at court, but because they were of a less distinguished branch of the famous Minamoto clan than Kitabatake's own Murakami branch. In particular, he disliked [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], the first Ashikaga shogun, who had originally supported Go-Daigo's claim to the Throne, but who ultimately headed the Northern Court and sought to destroy all who supported the Emperor's Southern Court.{{rp|30–31}} Kitabatake also wrote of [[Nitta Yoshisada]], one of the greatest military supporters of Go-Daigo, in a bad light. He saw Nitta as a warrior, little different from the Shoguns, but also accused him of never being available when the Emperor called upon him. Had Nitta been more available in the south of the country, instead of fighting his own battles in the north, Kitabatake claims, Nitta could have prevented the death of his son [[:en:Kitabatake Akiie|Akiie]].{{rp|65}} When Go-Daigo died in 1339, Kitabatake was under siege in his stronghold in [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]]. He sent copies of his major works to the new emperor, twelve-year-old [[:en:Emperor Go-Murakami|Emperor Go-Murakami]], advising him and his advisors.{{rp|67}} Though he is most well known for his writings and his role as Imperial advisor, Kitabatake was also a competent commander in battle, and held off the superior forces of the shogunate on many occasions. The [[siege of Hitachi]] lasted four years, and though his fortress ultimately fell to the supporters of the shogunate, Kitabatake escaped to [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], the capital of the Southern Court, where he advised the Emperor until his own death in 1354.{{rp|69,84,87–88,97}} ==Works== In his writings on the legitimate imperial lineage, Kitabatake delved into the subject of land ownership. In one document he denounces the Provincial [[:en:Shugo|Shugo]] and [[:en:Jitō|Jitō]] (Constables and Stewards) as land-hungry, and writes that the creation of this system in dd90 altered the traditional state of the country, and caused it to lose the art of government.{{rp|23}} Though the full details of his ideas for reform are unknown, it is likely that Kitabatake sought a return to the governmental structures set down during the [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō Era]], in 702, before the rise of feudalism and military rule. He recognized that certain privileges gained by the ''bushi'' (great warrior families) and ''[[kuge]]'' (court nobility) in that time would not be relinquished, but he did seek to abolish the systems of land tenure and tax collection that supported the power of the ''bushi''. Kitabatake viewed the ''bushi'', and by extension the ''bakufu'' ([[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|shogunate]]), the military government, as enemies of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|throne]].{{rp|23}} In 1339, he wrote ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'', a chronicle which relates the history of Japan through analysis of the Imperial reigns, from the earliest legendary semi-mythical emperors down to Daigō II (Emperor Go-Daigo) and his successor Murakami II (Emperor Go-Murakami). It was intended largely as a guide to the young Murakami, and as a treatise defending the Southern Court's legitimacy. It was largely written on the road, and recompiled and edited in 1343.{{rp|67–68}} One of the issues it addressed was the chaotic and unbalanced distribution of land, which he blamed on the government. But he also blamed the government officials and feudal lords who claimed the land. He wrote that seeking rewards was not part of proper behavior, and that it was a warrior's place to give up land and even his life for duty. He also claimed that the chaos of feudalism was derived, ultimately, from an unlimited number of people claiming a limited amount of land.{{rp|30}} Kitabatake's other major work, ''Shokugen-shō'', was also written in 1339, largely from memory, as the author, under siege in his home province, was unable to perform research at Court. It described the origins and organization of governmental offices and structures, as well as the author's opinions on the promotion and appointment of officials.{{rp|68}} ==Legacy== Kitabatake is considered one of the greatest men of his time, along with his rival [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]].{{rp|97–98,102–106}} Though his works are heavily colored by his personal bias and political motives, they are some of the most detailed accounts available on the history of Japan's feudal government and Imperial line. ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] (April 2, 1908; posthumous) ==See also== * [[Kitabatake clan]] * [[Abeno Shrine]] * [[Tenryū-ji]] * [[:en:Prince Kaneyoshi|Prince Kaneyoshi]], contemporary ally ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; {{OCLC|51096469}} * McGready, Ian, ed. (1995). ''Great Thinkers of the Eastern World.'' (H. Paul Varley, "Kitabatake Chikafusa."). New York: [[:en:HarperCollins|HarperCollins]]. {{ISBN|0-06-270085-5}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=0gweAAAAMAAJ ''Sovereign and Subject.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1014075}} * [[:en:George Bailey Sansom|Sansom]], George. (1961). [https://books.google.com/books?id=ffy6HAAACAAJ&q=A+History+of+Japan,+1334-1615 ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.''] Stanford: Stanford University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-8047-0525-7}}; {{OCLC|43483194}} [[en:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[arz:كيتاباتاكى شيكافوسا]] [[de:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[es:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[fa:کیتاباتاکه چیکافوسا]] [[fr:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[it:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[ja:北畠親房]] [[ko:기타바타케 지카후사]] [[pl:Chikafusa Kitabatake]] [[pt:Kitabatake Chikafusa]] [[zh:北畠親房]] {{wikidata link|Q683792}} [[Category:1354 deaths]] [[Category:14th-century Japanese historians]] [[Category:dd 6 4]] [[Category:Deified Japanese men]] [[Category:Kuge]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
